title: Merck halts ph. 2 Alzheimer's trial after Neuphoria small molecule underwhelms
summary: Merck & Co. has terminated its phase 2 trial of MK-1167, a small molecule alpha-7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor positive allosteric modulator (PAM), after an interim analysis failed to demonstrate sufficient efficacy in improving Alzheimer's dementia symptoms when paired with a standard acetylcholinesterase inhibitor. The 349-patient study was halted for efficacy — not safety — reasons, with Merck stating the drug did not meet criteria warranting further investigation. This marks another significant blow for Neuphoria Therapeutics, which is already reeling from its social anxiety candidate BNC210 (soclenicant) failing a phase 3 trial in October and an unsuccessful attempted buyout. Neuphoria is now considering a merger with London-based Scancell Holdings proposed just two days ago. With the MK-1167 trial sidelined, only one other Alzheimer's candidate remains in Merck's pipeline: the tau-targeting monoclonal antibody MK-2214 (developed by Teijin Pharma), currently in phase 2 and set to complete in 2029.

[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Merck halts ph. 2 Alzheimer’s trial after Neuphoria small molecule underwhelms

By
[Darren Incorvaia](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/darren-incorvaia) Jul 1, 2026 2:35pm

[Merck & Co.](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/merck-co) [Alzheimer's disease](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/alzheimers-disease-0) [failed trials](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/failed-trials) [Clinical Trial Results](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/clinical-trial-results)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/merck-halts-ph-2-alzheimers-trial-after-neuphoria-small-molecule-underwhelms#)

Merck & Co. has nixed the phase 2 study of a potential Alzheimer’s disease medicine after the drug underwhelmed in an interim analysis, marking another blow for the pharma’s struggling partner Neuphoria Therapeutics.

Merck stopped the [349-patient study](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06721156) after the small molecule MK-1167 “did not meet the necessary efficacy criteria to warrant further investigation,” a company spokesperson told Fierce Biotech. The trial was testing the ability of MK-1167, a modulator of the alpha-7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, to improve Alzheimer’s dementia symptoms when paired with a standard acetylcholinesterase inhibitor.

The trial was not terminated for any safety reasons, the spokesperson added.

MK-1167 isn’t currently being evaluated in any other clinical trials, but the Neuphoria collaboration still stands, according to the Merck spokesperson.

Neuphoria has been a longtime partner of Merck, with the two [first pairing up](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/bionomics-and-merck-enter-new-research-collaboration-264316911.html) back in 2014 when Neuphoria was still based in Australia and known as Bionomics. The biotech [rebranded](https://ir.neuphoriatx.com/news-releases/news-release-details/bionomics-limited-announces-intention-re-domicile-united-0) and moved to the U.S. in 2024.

Merck launched the phase 2 trial of MK-1167 in February 2025, which triggered a $15 million [milestone payment](https://ir.neuphoriatx.com/news-releases/news-release-details/neuphoria-therapeutics-inc-receive-15m-milestone-payment-merck) for Neuphoria. That payment was a bright spot in an otherwise difficult year for the neuro outfit; in October, the company’s social anxiety candidate [failed a phase 3 trial](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/neuphoria-nixes-social-anxiety-program-and-starts-strategic-review-after-ph-3-flop), sparking a strategic review and an [attempted buyout](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/neuphoria-reeling-phase-3-trial-fail-mulls-buyout-offer-investor) by an investor that ultimately didn’t go through.

Just two days ago, London-based Scancell Holdings [proposed a merger](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/uk-based-scancell-bids-nasdaq-listed-neuphoria-increase-profile-us-investors) with Neuphoria that the latter is [now considering](https://ir.neuphoriatx.com/news-releases/news-release-details/neuphoria-responds-announcement-made-london-listed-scancell).

That failed social anxiety candidate, BNC210 or soclenicant, is a negative modulator that tunes down the alpha-7 receptor. MK-1167 is instead a positive allosteric modulator (PAM), meaning it binds to the receptor and amps up its activity.

With the MK-1167 trial sidelined, that leaves just one other Alzheimer’s candidate in Merck’s pipeline, according to its [website](https://www.merck.com/research/product-pipeline/). That drug, a monoclonal antibody targeting tau called MK-2214, is currently in a [phase 2 trial](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT07033494) set to wrap up in 2029. The antibody was developed by Japan’s Teijin Pharma and [licensed](https://www.merck.com/news/merck-enters-exclusive-worldwide-license-agreement-with-teijin-pharma-for-investigational-antibody-candidate-targeting-tau/) by Merck in 2017.

title: Endevica spinout Kalohexis submits confidential IPO filing to develop obesity challenger to GLP-1s
summary: Kalohexis, a clinical-stage metabolic biotech spun out of privately held Endevica Bio in March 2026, has confidentially submitted an IPO registration statement to the SEC. The company's portfolio centers on melanocortin system-targeted peptides: lead asset 710GO is an oral dual MC3R/MC4R agonist in Phase 1 for weight loss, while second candidate mifomelatide targets muscle-wasting cancer cachexia in Phase 2 as an injectable MC3R/MC4R antagonist. Endevica Bio's CEO Russell Potterfield leads Kalohexis following his co-founding of the parent company in 2015. Clinical-stage lead Daniel Marks claims preclinical data shows 710Go avoids key GLP-1 side effects—including lean mass loss, GI distress, and rebound weight gain—by instead shifting the body's metabolic set-point rather than merely suppressing appetite. The confidential filing lands amid a resurgent biotech IPO window exemplified by Kailera Therapeutics' recent $625M offering.

[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Endevica spinout Kalohexis submits confidential IPO filing to develop obesity challenger to GLP-1s

By
[Will Maddox](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox) Jul 7, 2026 10:21am

[Obesity](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/obesity) [biotech IPO](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biotech-ipo) [GLP-1](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/glp-1) [Kalohexis](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/kalohexis)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/metabolic-biotech-kalohexis-files-confidential-ipo-develop-glp-1-challenger#)

Clinical-stage biotech Kalohexis is preparing for a public listing to support its obesity and cancer cachexia treatments.

Though the company has not determined how many shares it plans to offer or the price, Kalohexis revealed in a July 7 [release](https://www.kalohexis.com/kalohexis-announces-confidential-submission-of-draft-registration-statement-for-proposed-initial-public-offering/) that it has confidentially submitted the relevant IPO documents to the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Kalohexis [spun out of](https://www.endevicabio.com/endevica-announces-spinout-of-kalohexis-to-advance-first-and-best-in-class-therapeutics-harnessing-the-melanocortin-system-to-treat-obesity-and-metabolic-diseases/) the privately owned peptide therapeutic company Endevica Bio in March to focus on treatments that leverage the body’s melanocortin system, which regulates metabolic homeostasis. Kalohexis has developed its own peptide candidates that target central melanocortin-3 and -4 receptors (MC3R/MC4R) to treat a range of metabolic disorders.

The biotech's lead assets are 710GO, an oral dual MC3R/MC4R agonist in phase 1 development for weight loss, and mifomelatide, an injectable dual MC3R/MC4R antagonist in phase 2 for the treatment of a wasting syndrome called cachexia in patients with advanced cancers. The company also has several preclinical discovery programs.

Back when the biotech spun out of Endevica, Kalohexis Chief Medical and Scientific Officer Daniel Marks, M.D., Ph.D., said the company's approach has shown advantages over GLP-1s in the obesity space.

“710GO has the potential to be a healthier, more durable obesity treatment than current standard of care because rather than just suppressing appetite, it changes the body's metabolic set-point,” Marks said at the time.

Marks also pointed to preclinical data that he claimed showed 710GO “did not cause the side effects common with GLP-1 receptor agonists, such as loss of lean body mass, gastrointestinal distress and rapid weight regain following treatment cessation.”

Kalohexis is led by Russell Potterfield, who has served as Endevica's CEO since 2020 after helping found the company in 2015.

Kalohexis' leadership will likely take heart from the renewed investor interest in biotech IPOs this year, which has included the [$625 million offering](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/kailera-raises-head-turning-625m-ipo-fund-obesity-pipeline) by obesity-focused Kailera Therapeutics.

title: Novartis to pay $1.1B upfront for UK biotech Myricx to expand the pharma's ADC pipeline
summary: Novartis has agreed to acquire London-based Myricx Bio for $1.1 billion upfront plus up to $400 million in performance milestones, marking the company's most aggressive entry into the antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) space. Myricx's platform delivers N-myristoyltransferase inhibitor (NMTi) payloads via ADCs targeting HER2, TROP2, and B7-H3; preclinical data in Nature Genetics demonstrated that NMTis selectively eliminate senescent cells, while posters presented at World Journal of Clinical Drugs showed these ADCs matched or exceeded topoisomerase I-based competitors in solid tumor models. The lead programs are B7-H3 and HER2-directed ADCs, putting Myricx in direct competition with approved assets such as AstraZeneca/Daiichi Sankyo's Enhertu (HER2, $2.8B sales) and Merck & Co./Daiichi's ifinatamab deruxtecan (B7-H3, undergoing priority review). The deal signals Novartis' strategic pivot from relying solely on radioligand therapies toward building a broader ADC portfolio built around the novel NMTi payload class, with regulatory success serving as the gateway to expand NMTi-ADCs across additional targets.
raw_markdown:
[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Novartis to pay $1.1B upfront for UK biotech Myricx to expand the pharma's ADC pipeline

By
[Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor) Jul 6, 2026 5:10am

[Novartis](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/novartis) [Myricx Bio](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/myricx-bio) [mergers and acquisitions](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/mergers-and-acquisitions) [antibody-drug conjugates](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/antibody-drug-conjugates)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/novartis-pay-11b-upfront-myricx-expand-adc-pipeline#)

Novartis is pushing deeper into antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) development, [paying](https://www.novartis.com/news/media-releases/novartis-agrees-acquire-myricx-bio-advancing-next-generation-antibody-drug-conjugate-innovation-novel-nmti-payload-expanding-options-cancer-patients) $1.1 billion upfront to buy Myricx Bio for a pipeline based on a novel payload.

Switzerland’s Novartis largely stayed on the sidelines as its peers plowed into the ADC space. Talking to investors in recent years, Novartis executives explained that they have internal ADC projects and keep tabs on external developments. But the company made targeted radioligand therapies (RLTs), not ADCs, the cornerstone of its oncology business.

Now, Novartis has found a platform compelling enough to get more actively involved in ADCs. Myricx, a London-based biotech, is developing ADCs that deliver N-myristoyltransferase inhibitor (NMTi) payloads.

The FDA has approved ADCs using a range of payloads. Drugs including AstraZeneca and Daiichi Sankyo’s Enhertu deliver topoisomerase I (TOPO-1) inhibitors, while ADCs such as Astellas and Pfizer’s Padcev use microtubule inhibitors. NMTis are a different approach that Novartis predicts could tackle resistance and other limitations of existing payloads, broadening the use of ADCs across multiple tumor types.

Myricx and its collaborators [found](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41556-023-01287-6) NMTis selectively eliminate senescent cells, which can facilitate tumor progression. In solid cancer models, NMTi-ADCs aimed at HER2, TROP2 and B7-H3 [matched](https://myricxbio.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/ADC-world-Oct-2023-WADC-poster.pdf) (PDF) or beat the efficacy of TOPO-1 treatments aimed at the same targets.

The biotech picked the B7-H3 and HER2 ADCs as its lead candidates. Pursuing the targets puts Myricx in competition with multiple other ADCs, including approved assets. The FDA approved the HER2-directed ADC Enhertu in 2019, with AstraZeneca [reporting](https://www.astrazeneca.com/content/dam/az/PDF/2025/Q4-FY/Full-year-Q4-2025-results-announcement.pdf)(PDF) sales of $2.8 billion last year. Daiichi and Merck & Co.’s B7-H3-directed ADC ifinatamab deruxtecan is [undergoing](https://daiichisankyo.us/press-releases/-/article/ifinatamab-deruxtecan-granted-priority-review-in-the-us-for-adult-patients-with-previously-treated-extensive-stage-small-cell-lung-cancer-who-experienced-disease-progression-on-or-after-platinum-based-chemotherapy) priority review at the FDA.

With rivals showing B7-H3 and HER2 are viable ADC targets, Myricx’s programs are partly derisked. The biotech knows ADCs against B7-H3 and HER2 can improve outcomes in solid tumor patients. The critical outstanding question is whether Myricx’s technology can improve on the incumbent drugs, potentially by treating TOPO-1-resistant patients or unlocking settings where existing payloads have limitations.

The B7-H3 and HER2 programs could serve as a proving ground for NMTis. If it clinically validates the technology, Novartis sees opportunities to establish NMTis as a new class of ADC payload for use across more targets and platforms. Up to $400 million in milestones are tied to the success of Myricx’s assets.

The acquisition aligns with comments Shiva Malek, Ph.D., global head, oncology disease area at Novartis Institutes for BioMedical Research, [made](https://www.novartis.com/events/meet-novartis-management-2025) at the company’s “meet the management” day in November. The pharma is focused on selectively delivering “biology-matched payloads” to tumors, Malek said at the time. Within that work, Novartis was considering where pan-cytotoxic ADCs may complement its work on RLTs.

title: Roche orchestrates phase 3 KRAS lung cancer win over Amgen, BMS
summary: Roche's investigational KRAS G12C inhibitor divarasib has beaten Amgen's Lumakras (sotorasib) and Bristol Myers Squibb's Krazati (adagrasib) in the phase 3 Krascendo 1 trial, achieving statistically significant improvements in both progression-free survival and overall survival across 338 patients with previously treated, KRAS G12C-mutated non-small cell lung cancer. The results establish divarasib as a new standard of care in second-line NSCLC, according to Levi Garraway, Roche's chief medical officer. Despite Lumakras and Krazati being the first FDA-approved KRAS G12C inhibitors (2021 and 2022), both have posted subpar sales—$363M in 2024 for Lumakras and $205M in 2025 for Krazati—failing to capitalize on their headstart. Roche forecasts peak divarasib sales of CHF 1–2 billion ($1.2–2.5B) and has a regulatory filing planned for 2027 approval in second-line NSCLC, with additional trials running in first-line metastatic and adjuvant settings.
raw_markdown:
[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Roche orchestrates phase 3 KRAS lung cancer win over Amgen, BMS

By
[Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor) Jul 2, 2026 8:30am

[Amgen](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/amgen) [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/bristol-myers-squibb) [Roche](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/roche) [Lumakras](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/lumakras)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/roche-orchestrates-phase-3-kras-lung-cancer-win-over-amgen-and-bristol-myers#)

Roche’s investigational KRAS G12C inhibitor has [beaten](https://www.roche.com/investors/updates/inv-update-2026-07-02) Amgen’s Lumakras and Bristol Myers Squibb’s Krazati in a phase 3 trial, furthering its ambitions to establish divarasib as a new standard of care.

The open-label Krascendo 1 trial [enrolled](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06497556) 338 people with previously treated, KRAS G12C-mutated non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Patients who had already tried a KRAS G12C inhibitor or pan-KRAS/RAS inhibitor were excluded from the trial. Participants took either divarasib once daily, Lumakras once daily or Krazati twice daily.

Roche linked divarasib to statistically significant improvements in progression-free survival and overall survival, achieving the trial’s primary and key secondary endpoint. The results should establish divarasib as a new standard of care in the patient population, Levi Garraway, M.D., Ph.D., Roche’s chief medical officer, said in a statement. Roche has yet to share any survival numbers from the clinical trial.

The readout validates Roche’s argument that divarasib is a differentiated molecule. Divarasib showed better potency and selectivity than the incumbent KRAS G12C inhibitors during in vitro tests. After cross-trial comparisons [suggested](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/roches-kras-data-suggest-potential-edge-over-amgen-and-mirati-jury-remains-out) the edge translated into better clinical outcomes, Roche [axed](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/roche-culls-cough-candidate-pivots-kras-program-after-revaluing-entire-pipeline#:~:text=Roche%20also%20axed,or%20metastatic%20NSCLC.) a study comparing divarasib to the chemotherapy docetaxel and went toe-to-toe with Lumakras and Krazati.

Roche’s successful bet that divarasib would win a head-to-head study could help the KRAS G12C inhibitor come from behind to claim the second-line NSCLC market. The FDA approved Lumakras and Krazati in KRAS G12C-mutated, locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC in 2021 and 2022, respectively. Amgen and BMS have struggled to capitalize on their headstarts over Roche.

Lumakras and Krazati were scientific breakthroughs against a previously undruggable target, but sales have underwhelmed. Analysts [tipped](https://www.fiercepharma.com/marketing/brace-yourself-amgens-lumakras-sales-might-disappoint-key-data-readouts-draw-near-says#:~:text=For%20the%20full%20year%2C%20SVB%20also%20dialed%20down%20Lumakras%E2%80%99%20global%20sales%20estimate%20to%20%24326%20million%2C%20versus%20the%20Street%E2%80%99s%20%24347%20million%20forecast.) Lumakras to generate $347 million in 2022, its first full year on the U.S. market. Lumakras’ sales belatedly [passed](https://www.amgen.com/newsroom/press-releases/2025/02/amgen-reports-fourth-quarter-and-full-year-2024-financial-results) that target in 2024 and [reached](https://www.amgen.com/newsroom/press-releases/2026/02/amgen-reports-fourth-quarter-and-full-year-2025-financial-results) $363 million last year. BMS, which [acquired](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/bristol-myers-buys-mirati-58b-i-o-giant-branches-out-targeted-therapy-cancer) Krazati in a $5.8 billion deal, [reported](https://www.bms.com/assets/bms/us/en-us/pdf/investor-info/doc_financials/quarterly_reports/2025/BMY-Q4-2025-Earnings-Press-Release.pdf) $205 million in sales of the drug last year.

Undisclosed details of the divarasib data will shape whether Roche can deliver on its ambition to blast past its rivals’ sales. Roche [forecasts](https://assets.roche.com/f/176343/x/b992aea799/irp260423-a.pdf) (PDF) peak divarasib sales of 1 to 2 billion Swiss francs ($1.2 to $2.5 billion). The company has ongoing trials in first-line advanced or metastatic NSCLC and in an adjuvant lung cancer setting. Metastatic sales account for most of the forecast, Roche [said](https://www.roche.com/investors/events/asco-2026) at an event last month.

The company plans to present data at an upcoming medical meeting and submit the results to health authorities. Roche recently listed a filing for approval of divarasib in second-line NSCLC among planned submissions for 2027.

[Amgen](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/amgen) [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/bristol-myers-squibb) [Roche](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/roche) [Lumakras](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/lumakras) [Krazati](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/adagrasib) [KRAS](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/kras) [non-small cell lung cancer](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/non-small-cell-lung-cancer) [Clinical Data](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/clinical-data) [Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech) [Clinical Data](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/clinical-data)

title: Scribe notes down IPO ambition to fund lipid-lowering genetic meds through clinical trials
summary: Scribe Therapeutics has confidentially filed an IPO registration statement with the SEC to raise capital for advancing its portfolio of lipid-lowering genetic medicines. The Bay Area company emerged from Jennifer Doudna's UC Berkeley lab in 2020 and is now led by co-founder and CEO Benjamin Oakes, Ph.D. Its lead program STX-1150 is a gene-silencing therapeutic targeting PCSK9 for cardiovascular disease, currently in a first-in-human Phase 1 study in Australia with topline LDL-C data expected in H1 2027. Separately, the company is advancing TX-1200 (targeting LPA) and STX-1400 (targeting APOC3), both aimed at treating atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Scribe has $49.7 million in cash on balance sheet and 89 employees as of end-March 2026, but has also been pursuing larger partnerships with Sanofi and Eli Lilly after a prior $100M fundraise.
raw_markdown:
[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Scribe notes down IPO ambition to fund lipid-lowering genetic meds through clinical trials

By
[James Waldron](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/james-waldron) Jul 6, 2026 7:54am

[Scribe Therapeutics](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/scribe-therapeutics) [IPO](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/ipo) [biotech IPO](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biotech-ipo) [epigenetics](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/epigenetics)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/metabolic-biotech-kalohexis-files-confidential-ipo-develop-glp-1-challenger#)

![Heart](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/heart.jpg?VersionId=VztkRpG6fgIr0_YqVlPxJ8G5OnsEYrTx)Money from the IPO will also be used to advance TX-1200 and STX-1400, which target the LPA and APOC3 genes, respectively. (By sonmez/iStock/Getty Images Plus)

Scribe Therapeutics is the latest biotech to prepare a public listing, as the company looks to propel its lipid-lowering medicines through clinical trials.

While the Bay Area biotech has yet to set out how many shares it’s hoping to offer—or at what price—the company explained in a [July 2 filing](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1853921/000119312526294804/d21355ds1.htm) with the Securities and Exchange Commission that the proceeds would be used to push forward with its three key candidates.

Scribe [emerged](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/scribe-therapeutics-emerges-20m-biogen-pact-to-clear-crispr-hurdles) in 2020 with $20 million, a Biogen partnership and the backing of Jennifer Doudna, Ph.D., the CRISPR pioneer who [won a Nobel Prize](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/nobel-prize-chemistry-goes-to-crispr-pioneers-doudna-charpentier) the very next day. Benjamin Oakes, Ph.D.—now Scribe’s co-founder and CEO—joined Doudna’s lab to engineer new CRISPR molecules, eventually starting his own lab at the University of California, Berkeley, before transitioning his research into the startup Scribe.

The biotech’s goal is to bring genetic medicines out of the realm of rare diseases and into broader patient populations, Oakes [explained to Fierce](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/research/scribe-outlines-clinical-plans-after-crispr-candidates-lower-lipids-animals) last year. Doing so requires gene-editing enzymes that are safer and more potent.

To that end, Scribe has modified an enzyme called [CasX](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-019-0908-x), a variant of the better-known Cas9, to greatly improve its editing efficiency.

While CRISPR technology is best known for gene editing, Scribe’s lead program STX-1150 is instead a gene silencer. This means that rather than altering the genome, the compound adds methyl groups and tweaks the structure of DNA at the targeted gene, turning off gene expression. Scribe is aiming for STX-1150 to shut down the PCSK9 gene, a [known cardiovascular target](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0163725823001444) that is inhibited by approved cholesterol-lowering medicines like Amgen’s Repatha and Novartis’ Leqvio.

Oakes told Fierce back in November 2025 that he thinks of STX-1150 as the “end state” of RNA interference-style therapies, which includes Leqvio. While Leqvio is taken every six months, the Scribe CEO holds out hope that his company’s candidate could require a booster once every decade or two.

The company recently launched a first-in-human study of STX-1150 in Australia in up to 64 adults with elevated low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and increased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD). Topline data on whether the candidate can indeed trigger LDL-C-lowering activity are expected in the first half of 2027.

Money from the IPO will also be used to advance TX-1200 and STX-1400, which target the LPA and APOC3 genes, respectively, in order to treat ASCVD.

Meanwhile, Scribe has been pursuing other therapeutic areas in big-name partnerships with [Sanofi](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/sanofi-scribe-seal-new-vivo-partnership-worth-more-12-billion-eyes-treating-sickle-cell) and [Eli Lilly](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/lillys-prevail-inks-15b-pact-doudnas-scribe-crispr-tech), deals inked after the company secured a [$100 million fundraise](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/scribe-therapeutics-snags-100m-for-engineered-crispr-tech-pipeline) in 2021. The California biotech’s clinical push began in earnest in early 2025, with a [20% layoff](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/scribe-slims-down-workforce-it-readies-cardiometabolic-genetic-medicines-clinic) that followed an earlier preclinical data drop.

Scribe oversaw 89 full-time employees as of the end of March, with $49.7 million in the bank.

The company's potential path to the Nasdaq follows a boom period for biotech IPOs, including recent [record-breaking listings](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/after-two-record-setting-biotech-ipos-public-markets-are-back) from the likes of Kailera Therapeutics and Parabilis Medicines. Kardigan Therapeutics—which like, Scribe, is focused on cardiovascular disease— [grossed $400 million](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/kardigan-buttons-400m-upsized-ipo-power-heart-drugs-toward-phase-3) for its IPO last month.

# Whitehawk swoops in for five ADCs via development deal with China's Biocytogen

By [Will Maddox](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox)Jul 7, 2026 10:40am

[discovery](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/discovery) [ADCs](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/adcs) [Whitehawk Therapeutics](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/whitehawk-therapeutics) [Biocytogen](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biocytogen)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/metabolic-biotech-kalohexis-files-confidential-ipo-develop-glp-1-challenger#)

![white hawk bird flying](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/GettyImages-689706916.jpg?VersionId=gk4VyOiKDLTqT.OzxdSrSAekfxLzluZC)Whitehawk will evaluate five Biocytogen-supplied bispecific antibodies and will have the option to advance any resulting candidates. (Getty Images)

Biocytogen and Whitehawk Therapeutics are joining forces to develop bispecific antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) using Biocytogen's proprietary discovery platform.

Whitehawk will evaluate five Biocytogen-supplied bispecific antibodies and will have the option to advance any resulting ADC candidates as part of Whitehawk's pipeline. The deal includes an upfront and potential milestone payments coming Biocytogen's way, although the July 7 [release](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260707485864/en/Biocytogen-and-Whitehawk-Therapeutics-Enter-Global-Collaboration-for-Bispecific-Antibody-ADC-Development) did not disclose the details.

Biocytogen's RenLite platform is designed to discover, screen and optimize bispecific antibodies for diverse target combinations, and the collaboration aims to identify ADC candidates with differentiated targeting profiles.

“This collaboration further expands the application of Biocytogen’s fully human bispecific antibodies in ADC development,” Biocytogen CEO Yuelei Shen, Ph.D., said in the release. “We look forward to supporting the efficient advancement of multiple programs by contributing high-quality antibody molecules and integrated research capabilities to identify differentiated therapeutic candidates.”

China-headquartered Biocytogen has extensive experience helping biotechs discover and develop ADCs, including a [$400 million biobucks deal with Ideaya](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/ideaya-eyeing-drug-combos-bags-option-biocytogen-bispecific-adc-400m-deal) and a pact with Sotio worth up to [$326 million](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/sotio-taps-biocytogen-create-bispecific-adcs-326m-deal), both signed in 2024.

Whitehawk CEO Dave Lennon, Ph.D., said the company expects to have new ADC INDs in the next 12-24 months. “Biocytogen’s established platform provides a robust framework for exploring this modality in combination with our ADC platform,” Lennon said in the release.

Aadi Bioscience [became Whitehawk](https://www.fiercepharma.com/marketing/aadi-bioscience-takes-flight-new-moniker-whitehawk-therapeutics) last year after pivoting from a commercial-stage biopharma to a preclinical biotech. At the time, the company announced its “evolution and focus on accelerating its portfolio of ADCs with speed, agility and precision.”

New Jersey-based Whitehawk already has [three ADCs](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/aadi-pulls-commercial-preclinical-pivot-buying-adcs-and-selling-approved-drug-reboot) licensed from WuXi Biologics and Chinese biotech Hangzhou DAC through a deal announced at the end of 2024 that included a $44 million upfront payment.

# Boehringer bags PVT’s viral vector platform for cancer vaccine R&D

By [Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor)Jul 7, 2026 10:35am

[Boehringer Ingelheim](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/boehringer-ingelheim) [cancer vaccine](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/cancer-vaccine) [Licensing deals](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/licensing-deals) [oncolytic virus](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/oncolytic-virus)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/metabolic-biotech-kalohexis-files-confidential-ipo-develop-glp-1-challenger#)

![Boehringer Ingelheim booth at AACR 2026](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/IMG_0607.jpg?VersionId=f1cEkVGTMY_BQ7kNGn.ML0T..gUhXKgr)The deal furthers Boehringer’s longstanding interest in oncolytic viruses. (Darren Incorvaia)

Boehringer Ingelheim has landed an exclusive license to Prime Vector Technologies’ (PVT) viral vector platform for developing next-generation cancer vaccines.

The deal, which [features](https://cdn.website-editor.net/s/be9a4740c19f4a54933c082ad19f51b3/files/uploaded/260707+Press+Release.pdf?Expires=1786017581&Signature=khJuGknd-XSD9ZKF-7FS~6Ns2Nvj9dz90LHkRxzuBqTDJbMdleO4PaZCyhtZwSYq9wZOK1KiqCTd84go8n97U6CcrVuF1844lZsHieu4oA2PDSxF3ir5RsxejqnrVOfOzaI2v6BE7RGMPW60XX9cmIL-0JiCaSnyMlkcJHudmRiVlIWmWBTK3SkxFwasjs-5~9da7OkmPoDvzpETy~qLKZArNqctSjSPJodYrR6zxwx25bh4JQRN4XhF5uQvA7iERRLmDln9xmVj6Pw-Zyzf1zSf0bs0ABvOFB5-RvfNSqQLEeLeRDRTQQRcaHYVS8sTtj~QZ15OhaMvWO6oNEwpZg__&Key-Pair-Id=K2NXBXLF010TJW) (PDF) undisclosed upfront and milestone payments, covers the use of PVT’s Orf virus (ORFV) platform. PVT has studied the platform across cancer and infectious diseases, including through a COVID-19 program that reached the clinic. Boehringer has secured rights to use the platform for cancer vaccines, leaving PVT free to explore infectious diseases and other applications.

Interest in using ORFV, a parapoxvirus that infects sheep and goats, to treat cancer dates back more than a decade. In 2012, researchers [called](https://www.cell.com/molecular-therapy-family/molecular-therapy/fulltext/S1525-0016(16)32538-2?_returnURL=https%3A%2F%2Flinkinghub.elsevier.com%2Fretrieve%2Fpii%2FS1525001616325382%3Fshowall%3Dtrue) ORFV “an ideal viral backbone for the development of a cancer therapeutic,” reflecting evidence that the virus is safe in humans, supports repeat dosing and triggers a potent immune response.

Yet other pathogens, such as herpes simplex virus (HSV), adenovirus and vaccinia virus, have played a bigger role in cancer drug development to date. Amgen’s Imlygic uses HSV, as does the Replimune asset that is weeks away from an FDA approval decision.

Manufacturing scalability and uncertainties regarding safety in humans have limited use of ORFV, PVT CEO Ralf Amann [said](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/rmv.70038) in a paper published last year. Amann and his co-author cited Prime-2-CoV, PVT’s COVID-19 vaccine candidate, as a program that has cleared the uncertainties. The COVID-19 program [generated](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-393X/12/11/1288) feasibility, safety and immunogenicity evidence and established a manufacturing process.

PVT’s cancer vaccine work has yet to reach the clinic. But Boehringer has seen enough promise to write a check, securing a chance to assess the platform as a standalone cancer vaccine and in combination with other pipeline programs.

The deal furthers Boehringer’s longstanding interest in oncolytic viruses. In 2016, the German drugmaker [secured](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/boehringer-nabs-up-to-eu210m-oncolytic-virus-option-deal-austrian-startup) an option to buy ViraTherapeutics. Boehringer [exercised](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/boehringer-buys-oncolytic-virus-startup-viratherapeutics) its option in 2018 and [began](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05155332?tab=study) a phase 1 trial of VSV-GP, also called BI 1831169, in 2022. The company [reported](https://www.annalsofoncology.org/article/S0923-7534(24)02605-X/fulltext) initial data in 2024. Boehringer continues to list VSV-GP in its clinical pipeline but recently suspended the phase 1 trial.

Boehringer kept ViraTherapeutics’ site in Austria after buying the biotech. In January, ViraTherapeutics [unveiled](https://www.viratherapeutics.com/media-latest-news#20260601) a facility expansion intended to support an expansion beyond the roughly 45 people that made up its team at that time.

# As US lawmakers increase scrutiny of Chinese trials, industry fears ‘huge distraction and expense’

By [Will Maddox](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox)Jul 7, 2026 10:30am

[Congress](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/congress) [Pfizer](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/pfizer) [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/bristol-myers-squibb) [China](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/china)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/metabolic-biotech-kalohexis-files-confidential-ipo-develop-glp-1-challenger#)

![China US flags](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/china_us.jpg?VersionId=b5xOdpEd2WsupwpDvxdbPiunCP7VBf3w)The chair of the U.S. House Select Committee on China sent letters to Pfizer, Merck & Co, Bristol Myers Squibb and AbbVie last week. (ugurhan/iStock/Getty Images Plus)

As U.S. lawmakers continue to scrutinize the consequences of China’s expanding role in biotech innovation, industry insiders have been left debating how they can navigate this shifting landscape—or even whether biotechs should abandon China altogether.

The latest complication comes from Rep. John Moolenaar (R-MI), chair of the U.S. House Select Committee on China, who fired off letters to four Big Pharmas last week. The allegations in the letters ranged from human rights violations in a specific region of the country to concerns about inadvertently aiding China’s military.

The letters are the latest salvo in the political debate about China’s growing prominence as a source of new drugs for U.S. companies. Other strategies to temper this growth have included an ongoing discussion about limiting investment in Chinese biotechs via an amendment to the [COINS Act](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/divisive-bill-seeks-add-biotech-industry-law-limiting-investment-chinese-industry).

“Most investors have viewed what China's done in terms of enabling a rapid path to clinical development and clinical proof of concept as positive,” Omar Khalil, managing director at early-stage life sciences VC firm Santé Ventures, told Fierce.

“On the other hand, the longer-term implications on U.S. competitiveness from a biotech standpoint and losing that capability here in the U.S. has created some polarization,” Khalil added. “Certainly, every investor I've talked to has been wrestling with it.”

Last week’s letters went out to four U.S.-headquartered Big Pharmas, namely [Pfizer](https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/glp-1-maker-lilly-funded-clinical-trials-at-chinese-military-sites), [Merck & Co](https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/keytruda-maker-merck-funded-clinical-trials-at-china-s-military-sites)., [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/select-committee-investigates-bristol-myers-squibb-for-clinical-trials-at-chinese-military-sites) and [AbbVie.](https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/botox-maker-abbvie-funded-clinical-trials-at-chinese-military-sites) In the letters, Moolenaar noted that, according to the federal trials database, these companies all operate clinical trials in the Xinjiang region.

The committee’s [letter](https://chrome-extension//efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/sites/evo-subsites/selectcommitteeontheccp.house.gov/files/evo-media-document/chairman-moolenaar-letter-to-bms-june-29_2026.pdf) to BMS described the autonomous Xinjiang territory in northwest China as the “epicenter of the \[Chinese government’s\] genocide targeting Uyghurs and other ethnic and religious minorities.” In 2021, Congress responded to the allegations of human rights abuses by passing the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act to prevent goods made with forced labor in that region from entering the country.

The committee’s letters also allege that some trial sites are at medical centers and hospitals affiliated with China’s military. These studies “raise significant questions related to how data developed through clinical trials at those hospitals could fuel the \[Chinese Communist Party’s\] military biotechnology research, experimentation and capability development,” Moolenaar wrote in his letter to Pfizer.

While Pfizer, BMS and AbbVie hadn’t responded to questions from Fierce about the letters by time of publication, Merck said in a statement that “patient safety and ethical integrity are the foundational priorities of our clinical research program worldwide.” The company also explained that it follows global human rights standards such as [Good Clinical Practice](https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/human-regulatory-overview/research-development/compliance-research-development/good-clinical-practice) (GCP) and the Council for International Organizations of Medical Sciences.

“We look forward to a collaborative discussion with the Committee on advancing U.S. biotechnology innovation and continuing to ensure ethical clinical trials worldwide,” Merck added.

While the letters detail dozens of trials conducted in the Xinjiang region by those four Big Pharmas, a quick search of the federal trials database shows that these companies are far from alone. Other major U.S.-headquartered drugmakers like Johnson & Johnson, [Eli Lilly](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04847557?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Lilly&rank=1), [Amgen](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06295679?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Amgen&rank=1) and [Regeneron](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04423718?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Regeneron&rank=1) appear to have done the same, as well as European Big Pharmas like [Bayer](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT03349684?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Bayer&rank=1) and [Novartis.](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04638647?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Novartis&rank=1)

Beyond these household names, it also appears that Chinese companies that have secured licensing deals with U.S. companies have been running trials in Xinjiang. They [include Hengrui](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT02868801?term=Hengrui&viewType=Card&locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&rank=1), which signed a [$15 billion licensing deal](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/bms-inks-15b-biobuck-deal-bag-hengrui-assets-tap-chinas-rd-speed) with BMS in May for 13 early-stage assets, as well as supplying the obesity therapies behind Kailera Therapeutics’ [record-breaking IPO](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/kailera-raises-head-turning-625m-ipo-fund-obesity-pipeline) in April.

However, Moolenaar explained in the letters that the current scrutiny does not stretch to early-stage clinical trials conducted by Chinese biotechs who have since licensed drugs to U.S. companies.

### ‘Wasting time and money’

Still, not all companies that have dealings in China have the resources to navigate these newly choppy waters, and the increased oversight risks limiting smaller companies’ ability to do business there.

A spokesperson from a biotech industry group—who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of political repercussions—pointed out that it’s easier for Big Pharmas to absorb the costs of responding to changing political winds relative to their smaller biotech counterparts.

“It is difficult for any business to deal with mass uncertainty and constant changing of goal posts and what's acceptable,” they told Fierce. “If every time you turn around there's a new restriction and you have to start all over, that's wasting time and money.”

Santé Ventures’ Khalil agreed that responding to the shifting political rhetoric is “going to be a huge distraction and a huge expense” for smaller drug developers who are focused on raising capital.

“It's one thing for these letters to go to a big company, but for biotech startups, their goal is trying to get to clinical proof of concept so they can survive,” he said.

“Spending a lot of time, effort and money \[to\] avoid legal implications for running those trials is going to be a huge distraction and a huge expense,” Khalil added. “One may question whether the savings that you expect from running the trial in China are even worth it.”

While Khalil said he isn’t aware of anyone definitively stopping dealing with China, the scrutiny raises the diligence bar for biotechs to be confident that their trial site won’t end up on a blacklist at some point down the line.

Khalil said he is already hearing conversations with other VC funders and small biotechs about what it would look like to move trials to Australia, Japan, Singapore or Europe.

“That's becoming more of an emerging dynamic, and I think letters like this will probably accelerate some of that thinking,” he said. “Instead of doing that initial clinical trial in China, does it make sense to do it in Australia, South Korea or Japan, where there will be less geopolitical risk?”

One of the major attractions of running trials in China is their relative speed and cost compared with the U.S. While the FDA is attempting to [speed up the domestic trial process,](https://xn--early-stage%20vc%20firm%20sant%20ventures%20managing%20director%20omar%20khalil%20told%20fierce%20biotech-4dj/) Khalil suggested that in the meantime the money will flow to where it can be spent most efficiently.

RA Capital Managing Partner Peter Kolchinsky, Ph.D., told Fierce that there are still plenty of options for those who want to do business ethically in the Chinese trial market.

"There are clinical trial sites in China that 1) abide by China’s own GCP standards, which are largely harmonized with FDA standards, and 2) are subject to inspection by the FDA,” Kolchinsky said.

“These therefore represent viable and ethical options for clinical development,” he added.

AI-driven drug discovery company Insilico has extensive dealings with Chinese biotechs and has bases in both China and the U.S. The company’s CEO and founder Alex Zhavoronkov, Ph.D., said that handcuffing innovation by limiting business in clinical trials is no way to get ahead. He also voiced skepticism that the Big Pharmas’ trials had anything to do with human rights abuses.

“Allegations of human rights violations are absolute nonsense,” Zhavoronkov told Fierce. “It's just a reason to explain to your own people why you are not making drugs cheaper and faster with a higher probability of success.”

“You cannot compete in the UFC by restricting certain punches,” he added. “If you want to compete with China, you need to compete on innovation, so you need to genuinely invent and deregulate.”

# Endevica spinout Kalohexis submits confidential IPO filing to develop obesity challenger to GLP-1s

By [Will Maddox](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox)Jul 7, 2026 10:21am

[Obesity](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/obesity) [GLP-1](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/glp-1) [biotech IPO](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biotech-ipo) [IPO](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/ipo)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/metabolic-biotech-kalohexis-files-confidential-ipo-develop-glp-1-challenger#)

![Measuring tape on top of cash](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/GettyImages-1426421880.jpg?VersionId=BcMFM8bcqIgEDhe6CcK3Qgj.GQ.qkl0i)Kalohexis spun out of Endevica Bio in March to focus on metabolic disease by leveraging the body’s melanocortin system. (iStock / Getty Images Plus)

Clinical-stage biotech Kalohexis is preparing for a public listing to support its obesity and cancer cachexia treatments.

Though the company has not determined how many shares it plans to offer or the price, Kalohexis revealed in a June 7 [release](https://www.kalohexis.com/kalohexis-announces-confidential-submission-of-draft-registration-statement-for-proposed-initial-public-offering/) that it has confidentially submitted the relevant IPO documents to the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Kalohexis [spun out of](https://www.endevicabio.com/endevica-announces-spinout-of-kalohexis-to-advance-first-and-best-in-class-therapeutics-harnessing-the-melanocortin-system-to-treat-obesity-and-metabolic-diseases/) privately owned peptide therapeutic company Endevica Bio in March to focus on treatments by leveraging the body’s melanocortin system, which regulates metabolic homeostasis. Kalohexis has developed its own peptide candidates that target central melanocortin-3 and -4 receptors (MC3R/MC4R) to treat a range of metabolic disorders.

The biotech's lead assets are 710GO, an oral dual MC3R/MC4R agonist in phase 1 development for weight loss, and mifomelatide, a injectable dual MC3R/MC4R antagonist in phase 2 for the treatment of a wasting syndrome called cachexia in patients with advanced cancers. The company also has several preclinical discovery programs.

Back when the biotech spun out of Endevica, Kalohexis Chief Medical and Scientific Officer Daniel Marks, M.D., Ph.D., said the company's approach has shown advantages over GLP-1s in the obesity space.

“710GO has the potential to be a healthier, more durable obesity treatment than current standard of care because rather than just suppressing appetite, it changes the body's metabolic set-point,” Marks said at the time.

Marks also pointed to preclinical data that he claimed showed 710GO “did not cause the side effects common with GLP-1 receptor agonists, such as loss of lean body mass, gastrointestinal distress and rapid weight regain following treatment cessation.”

Kalohexis is led by Russell Potterfield, who has served as Endevica's CEO since 2020 after helping found the company in 2015.

Kalohexis' leadership will likely take heart from the renewed investor interest in biotech IPOs this year, which has included the [$625 million offering](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/kailera-raises-head-turning-625m-ipo-fund-obesity-pipeline) by obesity-focused Kailera Therapeutics.

# Kailera’s oral GLP-1 asset sees 11% weight loss, hits goals in Chinese phase 3 obesity and diabetes trials

By [Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor)Jul 7, 2026 08:44am

[Obesity](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/obesity) [GLP-1](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/glp-1) [weight loss](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/weight-loss) [Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/jiangsu-hengrui-medicine)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/metabolic-biotech-kalohexis-files-confidential-ipo-develop-glp-1-challenger#)

![Yellow tape measure with a variety of weight loss medication on blue background](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/GettyImages-1348209410.jpg?VersionId=ULXrxYF8OKCZEpCYyowVdLQrz2EO2vmh)The readouts expand the evidence on one of two oral weight-loss drugs in development at Kailera. (iStock/Getty Images Plus)

Phase 3 trials of Kailera Therapeutics’ oral GLP-1 prospect have [hit](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/07/07/3323036/0/en/kailera-therapeutics-announces-positive-topline-data-from-two-hengrui-pharma-phase-3-clinical-trials-of-oral-small-molecule-glp-1-receptor-agonist-hrs-7535-kai-7535.html) their primary endpoints, positioning the biotech’s partner Hengrui Pharma to seek approval in obesity and diabetes in China.

Hengrui is developing the once-daily oral small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist HRS-7535, which Kailera calls KAI-7535, in Greater China. The Chinese biotech shared data on Tuesday from two phase 3 trials, one in adults with obesity or overweight, and one in adults with Type 2 diabetes. Kailera, which has rights to the asset outside Greater China, began a phase 2 trial in people with obesity or overweight in April.

Hengrui’s obesity study linked HRS-7535 to mean weight loss of up to 10.9% at Week 44, compared to 2.5% for placebo. Weight loss climbed to 11.1% at Week 50 in an ad hoc analysis. The analyses excluded data collected after premature treatment discontinuations or the use of drugs with an obvious effect on weight. Including those data, weight loss on the high dose of HRS-7535 slipped to 9.8% at Week 44.

The weight loss results are in the same ballpark as data on existing oral GLP-1 drugs, namely Eli Lilly’s Foundayo and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy pill. Lilly [linked](https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2026/220934Orig1s000lbl.pdf) (PDF) Foundayo to weight loss of up to 11.1% at Week 72. Novo [reported](https://www.novo-pi.com/wegovy.pdf) (PDF) 13.6% weight loss at Week 64.

At a Jefferies event last month, Kailera CEO Ronald Renaud predicted that most oral drugs “are going to have weight loss in a very tight band, somewhere between 10% and 15%.” With multiple drugs offering similar weight loss, safety and tolerability is “where the differentiation happens,” Renaud said.

Seventy per cent of patients taking HRS-7535 in the obesity trial had nausea, making it the most common treatment-emergent adverse event. The nausea rate on placebo was 16.2%. Meanwhile, vomiting affected up to 68.6% of people on HRS-7535, compared to 4.5% of participants on placebo. The diarrhea figures for HRS-7535 and placebo were 36.9% and 15.3%, respectively.

For comparison, Nausea, constipation, diarrhea and vomiting, the four most common adverse events in Lilly’s phase 3 program, affected 24% to 35% of participants. Novo [reported](https://pro.novonordisk.ae/multimedia-resources/articles-and-news/oral-semaglutide-25-mg-obesityweek-findings.html) nausea and vomiting rates of 46.6% and 30.9%, respectively, in a phase 3 trial of oral Wegovy.

Hengrui reported the obesity data alongside results from a phase 3 trial of HRS-7535 in people with Type 2 diabetes. Excluding data collected after premature treatment discontinuations or the use of other glucose-lowering medications, HRS-7535 achieved reductions in HbA1c—a measure of blood sugar—of up to 1.68%. HbA1c fell by 1.28% on dapagliflozin, an SGLT2 inhibitor that AstraZeneca sells as Farxiga.

The readouts expand the evidence on one of two oral weight-loss drugs in development at Kailera. The biotech is also advancing an oral peptide that targets GLP-1 and GIP. Kailera, which [raised](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/kailera-raises-head-turning-625m-ipo-fund-obesity-pipeline) an impressive $625 million in an IPO in April, could report topline phase 2 data on the oral candidates next year.

title: United Therapeutics pays $140M for thymic cell therapy startup
summary: United Therapeutics has acquired Thymmune Therapeutics for $140 million upfront plus up to $160 million in milestone payments through 2031, gaining a preclinical iPSC-derived thymic cell therapy platform. The lead candidate, THY-100, converts human induced pluripotent stem cells into functional thymic cells, shown in animals to regenerate a neo-thymus. Thymmune's near-term focus is congenital athymia, an ultra-rare disease affecting ~20 U.S. births yearly where patients lack a thymus and historically died by age three. The deal directly competes with Sumitomo Pharma's Rethymic (approved 2021 for the same condition), positioning United to pursue a potentially scalable alternative to surgically implant cultured thymus tissue. Beyond congenital athymia, THY-100's roadmap targets transplant tolerance, immune-mediated diseases, and age-related thymic invagination for enhanced longevity in older adults.

# United Therapeutics pays $140M for thymic cell therapy startup

By
[Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor) Jul 3, 2026 5:49am

[United Therapeutics](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/united-therapeutics) [mergers and acquisitions](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/mergers-and-acquisitions) [Cell & Gene Therapy](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/cell-gene-therapy) [induced pluripotent stem cells](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/induced-pluripotent-stem-cells)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/united-therapeutics-pays-140m-thymic-cell-therapy-startup#)

United Therapeutics has [paid](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260702920280/en/United-Therapeutics-Corporation-Acquires-Thymmune-Therapeutics) $140 million to buy Thymmune Therapeutics for preclinical regenerative cell therapies focused on the thymus.

The thymus is the organ in the chest that regulates and develops T cells, key fighters in the body’s battles against infection and disease. As people age, the organ becomes smaller and less active. Thymmune has talked up the potential for its technology to reverse the decline, equipping people to fight off cancer and infection, but its near-term focus is on people with a rare disease who are born without a thymus.

United has paid the upfront fee and committed up to $160 million in milestones to buy the technology. The potential future payments are tied to clinical and regulatory milestones that United could pass as it advances the technology through 2031.

Thymmune’s innovation is a process for converting human-induced pluripotent stem cells into thymic cells. Once in the body, the thymic cells could mature into cell types and restore healthy T-cell function. In animals, Thymmune’s lead candidate, THY-100, triggered the formation of a “neo-thymus.”

THY-100 is in preclinical development for congenital athymia. Patients with the ultra-rare disease lack a thymus, making them vulnerable to fatal infections and immune dysregulation. All 49 congenital athymia patients [tracked](https://www.rethymic.com/RETHYMIC_Prescribing_Information_English.pdf) (PDF) in a natural history population from 1991 to 2017 died by three years of age.

Sumitomo Pharma cited the natural history study when seeking FDA approval for Rethymic, which came to market in 2021. The survival rate was 77% one year after treatment with Rethymic. Among patients alive at Year 1, the survival rate was 94% after a median follow-up of 10.7 years. Rethymic is made of cultured thymus tissue that is surgically implanted.

THY-100 is potentially more scalable. The rarity of congenital athymia, which is [estimated](https://primaryimmune.org/understanding-primary-immunodeficiency/types-of-pi/congenital-athymia) to affect about 20 live births a year in the U.S., means scalability may be less important in the ultra-rare disease than in other conditions. But United’s plans for the technology go beyond congenital athymia, with transplant tolerance, serious immune-mediated diseases and enhanced longevity for older adults on its roadmap.

The areas United could pursue if THY-100 achieves clinical proof of concept overlaps with the drug developer’s existing areas of activity. United is developing genetically engineered pig kidneys to offer an alternative to people on donor waiting lists, as well as regenerative medicines for conditions including end-stage renal disease and acute liver failure.

title: As US lawmakers increase scrutiny of Chinese trials, industry fears 'huge distraction and expense'
summary: Rep. John Moolenaar, chair of the House Select Committee on China, sent letters to Pfizer, Merck, Bristol Myers Squibb, and AbbVie alleging they operated clinical trials in China's Xinjiang region at sites affiliated with the Chinese military, raising concerns about human rights abuses and data potentially fueling Beijing's military biotech capabilities. The letters follow an ongoing congressional effort to curb US reliance on Chinese clinical research, including proposed amendments to the COINS Act limiting investment in Chinese biotechs. Industry respondents warned that geopolitical scrutiny will impose a 'huge distraction and expense' particularly for smaller startups already struggling to reach clinical proof of concept, as they face shifting compliance requirements and potential trial site blacklists. Some investors are exploring relocation of trials to Australia, Japan, Singapore, and Europe where geopolitical risk is lower, though Merck defended its programs as compliant with FDA Good Clinical Practice standards. The tension highlights a broader industry dilemma: China offers faster, cheaper trial execution but increasingly precarious political exposure.
raw_markdown:
[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# As US lawmakers increase scrutiny of Chinese trials, industry fears ‘huge distraction and expense’

By
[Will Maddox](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox) Jul 7, 2026 10:30am

[China](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/china) [Congress](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/congress) [Pfizer](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/pfizer) [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/bristol-myers-squibb)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/us-lawmakers-increase-scrutiny-chinese-trials-industry-fears-huge-distraction-and-expense#)

As U.S. lawmakers continue to scrutinize the consequences of China’s expanding role in biotech innovation, industry insiders have been left debating how they can navigate this shifting landscape—or even whether biotechs should abandon China altogether.

The latest complication comes from Rep. John Moolenaar (R-MI), chair of the U.S. House Select Committee on China, who fired off letters to four Big Pharmas last week. The allegations in the letters ranged from human rights violations in a specific region of the country to concerns about inadvertently aiding China’s military.

The letters are the latest salvo in the political debate about China’s growing prominence as a source of new drugs for U.S. companies. Other strategies to temper this growth have included an ongoing discussion about limiting investment in Chinese biotechs via an amendment to the [COINS Act](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/divisive-bill-seeks-add-biotech-industry-law-limiting-investment-chinese-industry).

“Most investors have viewed what China's done in terms of enabling a rapid path to clinical development and clinical proof of concept as positive,” Omar Khalil, managing director at early-stage life sciences VC firm Santé Ventures, told Fierce.

“On the other hand, the longer-term implications on U.S. competitiveness from a biotech standpoint and losing that capability here in the U.S. has created some polarization,” Khalil added. “Certainly, every investor I've talked to has been wrestling with it.”

Last week’s letters went out to four U.S.-headquartered Big Pharmas, namely [Pfizer](https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/glp-1-maker-lilly-funded-clinical-trials-at-chinese-military-sites), [Merck & Co](https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/keytruda-maker-merck-funded-clinical-trials-at-china-s-military-sites)., [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/select-committee-investigates-bristol-myers-squibb-for-clinical-trials-at-chinese-military-sites) and [AbbVie.](https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/media/letters/botox-maker-abbvie-funded-clinical-trials-at-chinese-military-sites) In the letters, Moolenaar noted that, according to the federal trials database, these companies all operate clinical trials in the Xinjiang region.

The committee’s [letter](https://chrome-extension//efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/https://chinaselectcommittee.house.gov/sites/evo-subsites/selectcommitteeontheccp.house.gov/files/evo-media-document/chairman-moolenaar-letter-to-bms-june-29_2026.pdf) to BMS described the autonomous Xinjiang territory in northwest China as the “epicenter of the \[Chinese government’s\] genocide targeting Uyghurs and other ethnic and religious minorities.” In 2021, Congress responded to the allegations of human rights abuses by passing the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act to prevent goods made with forced labor in that region from entering the country.

The committee’s letters also allege that some trial sites are at medical centers and hospitals affiliated with China’s military. These studies “raise significant questions related to how data developed through clinical trials at those hospitals could fuel the \[Chinese Communist Party’s\] military biotechnology research, experimentation and capability development,” Moolenaar wrote in his letter to Pfizer.

While Pfizer, BMS and AbbVie hadn’t responded to questions from Fierce about the letters by time of publication, Merck said in a statement that “patient safety and ethical integrity are the foundational priorities of our clinical research program worldwide.” The company also explained that it follows global human rights standards such as [Good Clinical Practice](https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/human-regulatory-overview/research-development/compliance-research-development/good-clinical-practice) (GCP) and the Council for International Organizations of Medical Sciences.

“We look forward to a collaborative discussion with the Committee on advancing U.S. biotechnology innovation and continuing to ensure ethical clinical trials worldwide,” Merck added.

While the letters detail dozens of trials conducted in the Xinjiang region by those four Big Pharmas, a quick search of the federal trials database shows that these companies are far from alone. Other major U.S.-headquartered drugmakers, like Johnson & Johnson, [Eli Lilly](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04847557?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Lilly&rank=1), [Amgen](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06295679?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Amgen&rank=1) and [Regeneron](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04423718?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Regeneron&rank=1), appear to have done the same, as have European Big Pharmas like [Bayer](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT03349684?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Bayer&rank=1) and [Novartis.](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04638647?locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&viewType=Card&term=Novartis&rank=1)

Beyond these household names, it also appears that Chinese companies that have secured licensing deals with U.S. companies have been running trials in Xinjiang. They [include Hengrui](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT02868801?term=Hengrui&viewType=Card&locStr=Xinjiang,%20China&country=CN&state=Xinjiang&rank=1), which signed a [$15 billion licensing deal](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/bms-inks-15b-biobuck-deal-bag-hengrui-assets-tap-chinas-rd-speed) with BMS in May for 13 early-stage assets, as well as supplying the obesity therapies behind Kailera Therapeutics’ [record-breaking IPO](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/kailera-raises-head-turning-625m-ipo-fund-obesity-pipeline) in April.

However, Moolenaar explained in the letters that the current scrutiny does not stretch to early-stage clinical trials conducted by Chinese biotechs who have since licensed drugs to U.S. companies.

### ‘Wasting time and money’

Still, not all companies that have dealings in China have the resources to navigate these newly choppy waters, and the increased oversight risks limiting smaller companies’ ability to do business there.

A spokesperson from a biotech industry group—who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of political repercussions—pointed out that it’s easier for Big Pharmas to absorb the costs of responding to changing political winds relative to their smaller biotech counterparts.

“It is difficult for any business to deal with mass uncertainty and constant changing of goal posts and what's acceptable,” they told Fierce. “If every time you turn around there's a new restriction and you have to start all over, that's wasting time and money.”

Santé Ventures’ Khalil agreed that responding to the shifting political rhetoric is “going to be a huge distraction and a huge expense” for smaller drug developers who are focused on raising capital.

“It's one thing for these letters to go to a big company, but for biotech startups, their goal is trying to get to clinical proof of concept so they can survive,” he said.

“Spending a lot of time, effort and money \[to\] avoid legal implications for running those trials is going to be a huge distraction and a huge expense,” Khalil added. “One may question whether the savings that you expect from running the trial in China are even worth it.”

While Khalil said he isn’t aware of anyone definitively stopping dealings with China, the scrutiny raises the diligence bar for biotechs to be confident that their trial sites won’t end up on a blacklist at some point down the line.

Khalil said he is already hearing conversations with other VC funders and small biotechs about what it would look like to move trials to Australia, Japan, Singapore or Europe.

“That's becoming more of an emerging dynamic, and I think letters like this will probably accelerate some of that thinking,” he said. “Instead of doing that initial clinical trial in China, does it make sense to do it in Australia, South Korea or Japan, where there will be less geopolitical risk?”

One of the major attractions of running trials in China is their relative speed and cost compared with the U.S. While the FDA is attempting to [speed up the domestic trial process,](https://xn--early-stage%20vc%20firm%20sant%20ventures%20managing%20director%20omar%20khalil%20told%20fierce%20biotech-4dj/) Khalil suggested that in the meantime the money will flow to where it can be spent most efficiently.

RA Capital Managing Partner Peter Kolchinsky, Ph.D., told Fierce that there are still plenty of options for those who want to do business ethically in the Chinese trial market.

"There are clinical trial sites in China that 1) abide by China’s own GCP standards, which are largely harmonized with FDA standards, and 2) are subject to inspection by the FDA,” Kolchinsky said.

“These therefore represent viable and ethical options for clinical development,” he added.

AI-driven drug discovery company Insilico has extensive dealings with Chinese biotechs and has bases in both China and the U.S. The company’s CEO and founder Alex Zhavoronkov, Ph.D., said that handcuffing innovation by limiting business in clinical trials is no way to get ahead. He also voiced skepticism that the Big Pharmas’ trials had anything to do with human rights abuses.

“Allegations of human rights violations are absolute nonsense,” Zhavoronkov told Fierce. “It's just a reason to explain to your own people why you are not making drugs cheaper and faster with a higher probability of success.”

“You cannot compete in the UFC by restricting certain punches,” he added. “If you want to compete with China, you need to compete on innovation, so you need to genuinely invent and deregulate.”

[China](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/china) [Congress](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/congress) [Pfizer](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/pfizer) [Bristol Myers Squibb](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/bristol-myers-squibb) [Merck & Co.](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/merck-co) [Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

title: Whitehawk swoops in for five ADCs via development deal with China's Biocytogen
summary: Whitehawk Therapeutics has entered a global collaboration agreement with China-based Biocytogen to discover and develop bispecific antibody-drug conjugates using Biocytogen's RenLite discovery platform. Whitehawk will evaluate five Biocytogen-supplied bispecific antibodies, with the option to advance any resulting ADC candidates into its pipeline; financial terms including upfront payments and milestones were not disclosed in the July 7 release. CEO Dave Lennon expects new ADC IND filings within 12–24 months. The deal builds on Biocytogen's proven track record: a $400 million collaboration with Ideaya and an up-to-$326 million pact with Sotio, both signed in 2024. Whitehawk, formerly Aadi Bioscience, has already licensed three ADCs from WuXi Biologics and Hangzhou DAC under a 2024 deal that included a $44 million upfront payment, signaling a strategic shift from commercial-stage operations to a preclinical ADC-focused biotech.
raw_markdown:
[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Whitehawk swoops in for five ADCs via development deal with China's Biocytogen

By
[Will Maddox](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox) Jul 7, 2026 10:40am

[Whitehawk Therapeutics](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/whitehawk-therapeutics) [Biocytogen](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biocytogen) [discovery](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/discovery) [ADCs](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/adcs)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/whitehawk-swoops-five-adcs-development-deal-biocytogen#)

Biocytogen and Whitehawk Therapeutics are joining forces to develop bispecific antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) using Biocytogen's proprietary discovery platform.

Whitehawk will evaluate five Biocytogen-supplied bispecific antibodies and will have the option to advance any resulting ADC candidates as part of Whitehawk's pipeline. The deal includes an upfront and potential milestone payments coming Biocytogen's way, although the July 7 [release](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260707485864/en/Biocytogen-and-Whitehawk-Therapeutics-Enter-Global-Collaboration-for-Bispecific-Antibody-ADC-Development) did not disclose the details.

Biocytogen's RenLite platform is designed to discover, screen and optimize bispecific antibodies for diverse target combinations, and the collaboration aims to identify ADC candidates with differentiated targeting profiles.

“This collaboration further expands the application of Biocytogen’s fully human bispecific antibodies in ADC development,” Biocytogen CEO Yuelei Shen, Ph.D., said in the release. “We look forward to supporting the efficient advancement of multiple programs by contributing high-quality antibody molecules and integrated research capabilities to identify differentiated therapeutic candidates.”

China-headquartered Biocytogen has extensive experience helping biotechs discover and develop ADCs, including a [$400 million biobucks deal with Ideaya](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/ideaya-eyeing-drug-combos-bags-option-biocytogen-bispecific-adc-400m-deal) and a pact with Sotio worth up to [$326 million](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/sotio-taps-biocytogen-create-bispecific-adcs-326m-deal), both signed in 2024.

Whitehawk CEO Dave Lennon, Ph.D., said the company expects to have new ADC INDs in the next 12-24 months. “Biocytogen’s established platform provides a robust framework for exploring this modality in combination with our ADC platform,” Lennon said in the release.

Aadi Bioscience [became Whitehawk](https://www.fiercepharma.com/marketing/aadi-bioscience-takes-flight-new-moniker-whitehawk-therapeutics) last year after pivoting from a commercial-stage biopharma to a preclinical biotech. At the time, the company announced its “evolution and focus on accelerating its portfolio of ADCs with speed, agility and precision.”

New Jersey-based Whitehawk already has [three ADCs](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/aadi-pulls-commercial-preclinical-pivot-buying-adcs-and-selling-approved-drug-reboot) licensed from WuXi Biologics and the Chinese biotech Hangzhou DAC, under a deal announced at the end of 2024 that included a $44 million upfront payment.

title: Caris Life Sciences launches cancer blood test 'Caris Detect'
summary: Caris Life Sciences has launched Caris Detect, a multi-cancer early detection (MCED) blood test that combines ultra-deep whole genome sequencing, whole transcriptome sequencing, and advanced AI to identify cancer at earlier stages. Based on the Caris Detect ACHIEVE 1 study, sensitivity increases with disease stage—56% for stage I, 67.7% for stage II, 79% for stage III, and 98.6% for stage IV—with overall stage I/II sensitivity at 60.3% across multiple tumor types including lung, breast, prostate, colorectal, skin, pancreas, head and neck, and uterus. The test targets the critical window of early-stage detection when cancers are typically asymptomatic but more easily treatable. Caris Detect joins two other already-marketed diagnostics: the FDA-cleared Cancer Seek assay (2024) for single-nucleotide variant analysis and the Caris Assure plasma blood test, enabling patient matching for targeted therapies from Novartis, Amgen, Pfizer, and Eli Lilly. Caris went public a year ago in a $494.1 million Nasdaq IPO and is currently valued at $5.3 billion.
